chapter: Chapter 2: Is Matter Around Us Pure

questions.question: Differentiate between a pure substance and a mixture.
questions.answer: A pure substance contains only one kind of particles with fixed composition, while a mixture contains two or more substances physically combined in any proportion.

questions.question: Give two examples each of homogeneous and heterogeneous mixtures.
questions.answer: Homogeneous mixtures: salt solution, air. Heterogeneous mixtures: oil and water, sand in water.

questions.question: Explain why a solution is considered a homogeneous mixture.
questions.answer: A solution is considered homogeneous because its composition is uniform throughout and the solute particles are evenly distributed in the solvent.

questions.question: List two properties of a true solution.
questions.answer: The particles are very small and cannot be seen with the naked eye, and the solute particles do not settle down on standing.

questions.question: Why are alloys considered homogeneous mixtures?
questions.answer: Alloys are considered homogeneous mixtures because their components are uniformly mixed and the composition is the same throughout.

questions.question: What happens when a saturated solution is heated?
questions.answer: When a saturated solution is heated, more solute can dissolve in it because solubility usually increases with temperature.

questions.question: Explain the term unsaturated solution.
questions.answer: An unsaturated solution is a solution in which more solute can still be dissolved at a given temperature.

questions.question: How can you distinguish between a solution and a suspension?
questions.answer: In a solution the particles are very small and do not settle down, whereas in a suspension the particles are large and settle down on standing.

questions.question: Mention two characteristics of suspensions.
questions.answer: The particles of a suspension are large and visible, and they settle down when the mixture is left undisturbed.

questions.question: Why do colloidal solutions show the Tyndall effect?
questions.answer: Colloidal solutions show the Tyndall effect because the particle size is large enough to scatter light.

questions.question: Give two examples of colloids.
questions.answer: Milk and fog are common examples of colloids.

questions.question: What is the role of dispersion medium in a colloid?
questions.answer: The dispersion medium is the substance in which the dispersed phase particles are distributed.

questions.question: Explain why filtration cannot separate dissolved substances in a solution.
questions.answer: Filtration cannot separate dissolved substances because the solute particles are too small and pass through the filter paper along with the solvent.

questions.question: How does centrifugation help in separating mixtures?
questions.answer: Centrifugation spins the mixture rapidly so that heavier particles settle at the bottom and lighter particles remain at the top.

questions.question: Explain the working principle of a separating funnel.
questions.answer: A separating funnel works on the principle of difference in densities of immiscible liquids, allowing the denser liquid to settle at the bottom and be drained first.

questions.question: Why is evaporation used to separate salt from seawater?
questions.answer: Evaporation removes the water as vapor, leaving the dissolved salt behind as a solid.

questions.question: What is the basic principle of chromatography?
questions.answer: Chromatography works on the principle that different substances travel at different speeds through a medium due to differences in solubility.

questions.question: Why is fractional distillation used for separating petroleum products?
questions.answer: Fractional distillation is used because petroleum contains several liquids with close boiling points that can be separated in fractions.

questions.question: What is the advantage of crystallization over evaporation?
questions.answer: Crystallization gives pure solid crystals and removes impurities better than evaporation.

questions.question: Give two examples of physical changes.
questions.answer: Melting of ice and cutting of paper are examples of physical changes.

questions.question: Give two examples of chemical changes.
questions.answer: Rusting of iron and burning of wood are examples of chemical changes.

questions.question: How are metals and non-metals classified as elements?
questions.answer: Elements are classified as metals and non-metals based on their physical and chemical properties such as conductivity, malleability, and reactivity.

questions.question: Explain why water is considered a compound.
questions.answer: Water is considered a compound because it is made of hydrogen and oxygen chemically combined in a fixed ratio of 2:1.

questions.question: State one difference between elements and compounds.
questions.answer: Elements consist of only one type of atom, while compounds contain two or more different elements chemically combined.

questions.question: Why can compounds not be separated by physical methods?
questions.answer: Compounds cannot be separated by physical methods because their components are chemically bonded together.
